units.text: Enter BERTRAM, the COUNTESS of Rousillon, HELENA, and LAFEU, all in black

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: In delivering my son from me, I bury a second husband.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: And I in going, madam, weep o'er my father's death
anew: but I must attend his majesty's command, to
whom I am now in ward, evermore in subjection.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: You shall find of the king a husband, madam; you,
sir, a father: he that so generally is at all times
good must of necessity hold his virtue to you; whose
worthiness would stir it up where it wanted rather
than lack it where there is such abundance.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: What hope is there of his majesty's amendment?

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: He hath abandoned his physicians, madam; under whose
practises he hath persecuted time with hope, and
finds no other advantage in the process but only the
losing of hope by time.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: This young gentlewoman had a father,--O, that
'had'! how sad a passage 'tis!--whose skill was
almost as great as his honesty; had it stretched so
far, would have made nature immortal, and death
should have play for lack of work. Would, for the
king's sake, he were living! I think it would be
the death of the king's disease.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: How called you the man you speak of, madam?

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: He was famous, sir, in his profession, and it was
his great right to be so: Gerard de Narbon.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: He was excellent indeed, madam: the king very
lately spoke of him admiringly and mourningly: he
was skilful enough to have lived still, if knowledge
could be set up against mortality.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: What is it, my good lord, the king languishes of?

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: A fistula, my lord.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: I heard not of it before.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: I would it were not notorious. Was this gentlewoman
the daughter of Gerard de Narbon?

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: His sole child, my lord, and bequeathed to my
overlooking. I have those hopes of her good that
her education promises; her dispositions she
inherits, which makes fair gifts fairer; for where
an unclean mind carries virtuous qualities, there
commendations go with pity; they are virtues and
traitors too; in her they are the better for their
simpleness; she derives her honesty and achieves her goodness.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Your commendations, madam, get from her tears.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: 'Tis the best brine a maiden can season her praise
in. The remembrance of her father never approaches
her heart but the tyranny of her sorrows takes all
livelihood from her cheek. No more of this, Helena;
go to, no more; lest it be rather thought you affect
a sorrow than have it.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I do affect a sorrow indeed, but I have it too.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Moderate lamentation is the right of the dead,
excessive grief the enemy to the living.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: If the living be enemy to the grief, the excess
makes it soon mortal.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Madam, I desire your holy wishes.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: How understand we that?

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Be thou blest, Bertram, and succeed thy father
In manners, as in shape! thy blood and virtue
Contend for empire in thee, and thy goodness
Share with thy birthright! Love all, trust a few,
Do wrong to none: be able for thine enemy
Rather in power than use, and keep thy friend
Under thy own life's key: be cheque'd for silence,
But never tax'd for speech. What heaven more will,
That thee may furnish and my prayers pluck down,
Fall on thy head! Farewell, my lord;
'Tis an unseason'd courtier; good my lord,
Advise him.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: He cannot want the best
That shall attend his love.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Heaven bless him! Farewell, Bertram.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: [To HELENA]  The best wishes that can be forged in
your thoughts be servants to you! Be comfortable
to my mother, your mistress, and make much of her.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Farewell, pretty lady: you must hold the credit of
your father.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: O, were that all! I think not on my father;
And these great tears grace his remembrance more
Than those I shed for him. What was he like?
I have forgot him: my imagination
Carries no favour in't but Bertram's.
I am undone: there is no living, none,
If Bertram be away. 'Twere all one
That I should love a bright particular star
And think to wed it, he is so above me:
In his bright radiance and collateral light
Must I be comforted, not in his sphere.
The ambition in my love thus plagues itself:
The hind that would be mated by the lion
Must die for love. 'Twas pretty, though plague,
To see him every hour; to sit and draw
His arched brows, his hawking eye, his curls,
In our heart's table; heart too capable
Of every line and trick of his sweet favour:
But now he's gone, and my idolatrous fancy
Must sanctify his reliques. Who comes here?
One that goes with him: I love him for his sake;
And yet I know him a notorious liar,
Think him a great way fool, solely a coward;
Yet these fixed evils sit so fit in him,
That they take place, when virtue's steely bones
Look bleak i' the cold wind: withal, full oft we see
Cold wisdom waiting on superfluous folly.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Save you, fair queen!

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: And you, monarch!

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: No.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: And no.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Are you meditating on virginity?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Ay. You have some stain of soldier in you: let me
ask you a question. Man is enemy to virginity; how
may we barricado it against him?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Keep him out.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: But he assails; and our virginity, though valiant,
in the defence yet is weak: unfold to us some
warlike resistance.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: There is none: man, sitting down before you, will
undermine you and blow you up.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Bless our poor virginity from underminers and
blowers up! Is there no military policy, how
virgins might blow up men?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Virginity being blown down, man will quicklier be
blown up: marry, in blowing him down again, with
the breach yourselves made, you lose your city. It
is not politic in the commonwealth of nature to
preserve virginity. Loss of virginity is rational
increase and there was never virgin got till
virginity was first lost. That you were made of is
metal to make virgins. Virginity by being once lost
may be ten times found; by being ever kept, it is
ever lost: 'tis too cold a companion; away with 't!

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I will stand for 't a little, though therefore I die a virgin.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: There's little can be said in 't; 'tis against the
rule of nature. To speak on the part of virginity,
is to accuse your mothers; which is most infallible
disobedience. He that hangs himself is a virgin:
virginity murders itself and should be buried in
highways out of all sanctified limit, as a desperate
offendress against nature. Virginity breeds mites,
much like a cheese; consumes itself to the very
paring, and so dies with feeding his own stomach.
Besides, virginity is peevish, proud, idle, made of
self-love, which is the most inhibited sin in the
canon. Keep it not; you cannot choose but loose
by't: out with 't! within ten year it will make
itself ten, which is a goodly increase; and the
principal itself not much the worse: away with 't!

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: How might one do, sir, to lose it to her own liking?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Let me see: marry, ill, to like him that ne'er it
likes. 'Tis a commodity will lose the gloss with
lying; the longer kept, the less worth: off with 't
while 'tis vendible; answer the time of request.
Virginity, like an old courtier, wears her cap out
of fashion: richly suited, but unsuitable: just
like the brooch and the tooth-pick, which wear not
now. Your date is better in your pie and your
porridge than in your cheek; and your virginity,
your old virginity, is like one of our French
withered pears, it looks ill, it eats drily; marry,
'tis a withered pear; it was formerly better;
marry, yet 'tis a withered pear: will you anything with it?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Not my virginity yet [         ]
There shall your master have a thousand loves,
A mother and a mistress and a friend,
A phoenix, captain and an enemy,
A guide, a goddess, and a sovereign,
A counsellor, a traitress, and a dear;
His humble ambition, proud humility,
His jarring concord, and his discord dulcet,
His faith, his sweet disaster; with a world
Of pretty, fond, adoptious christendoms,
That blinking Cupid gossips. Now shall he--
I know not what he shall. God send him well!
The court's a learning place, and he is one--

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: What one, i' faith?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: That I wish well. 'Tis pity--

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: What's pity?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: That wishing well had not a body in't,
Which might be felt; that we, the poorer born,
Whose baser stars do shut us up in wishes,
Might with effects of them follow our friends,
And show what we alone must think, which never
Return us thanks.

units.speaker: Page
lines: Monsieur Parolles, my lord calls for you.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Little Helen, farewell; if I can remember thee, I
will think of thee at court.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Monsieur Parolles, you were born under a charitable star.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Under Mars, I.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I especially think, under Mars.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Why under Mars?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: The wars have so kept you under that you must needs
be born under Mars.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: When he was predominant.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: When he was retrograde, I think, rather.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Why think you so?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: You go so much backward when you fight.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: That's for advantage.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: So is running away, when fear proposes the safety;
but the composition that your valour and fear makes
in you is a virtue of a good wing, and I like the wear well.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: I am so full of businesses, I cannot answer thee
acutely. I will return perfect courtier; in the
which, my instruction shall serve to naturalize
thee, so thou wilt be capable of a courtier's
counsel and understand what advice shall thrust upon
thee; else thou diest in thine unthankfulness, and
thine ignorance makes thee away: farewell. When
thou hast leisure, say thy prayers; when thou hast
none, remember thy friends; get thee a good husband,
and use him as he uses thee; so, farewell.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Our remedies oft in ourselves do lie,
Which we ascribe to heaven: the fated sky
Gives us free scope, only doth backward pull
Our slow designs when we ourselves are dull.
What power is it which mounts my love so high,
That makes me see, and cannot feed mine eye?
The mightiest space in fortune nature brings
To join like likes and kiss like native things.
Impossible be strange attempts to those
That weigh their pains in sense and do suppose
What hath been cannot be: who ever strove
So show her merit, that did miss her love?
The king's disease--my project may deceive me,
But my intents are fix'd and will not leave me.

units.text: Flourish of cornets. Enter the KING of France, with letters, and divers Attendants

units.speaker: KING
lines: The Florentines and Senoys are by the ears;
Have fought with equal fortune and continue
A braving war.

units.speaker: First Lord
lines: So 'tis reported, sir.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Nay, 'tis most credible; we here received it
A certainty, vouch'd from our cousin Austria,
With caution that the Florentine will move us
For speedy aid; wherein our dearest friend
Prejudicates the business and would seem
To have us make denial.

units.speaker: First Lord
lines: His love and wisdom,
Approved so to your majesty, may plead
For amplest credence.

units.speaker: KING
lines: He hath arm'd our answer,
And Florence is denied before he comes:
Yet, for our gentlemen that mean to see
The Tuscan service, freely have they leave
To stand on either part.

units.speaker: Second Lord
lines: It well may serve
A nursery to our gentry, who are sick
For breathing and exploit.

units.speaker: KING
lines: What's he comes here?

units.speaker: First Lord
lines: It is the Count Rousillon, my good lord,
Young Bertram.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Youth, thou bear'st thy father's face;
Frank nature, rather curious than in haste,
Hath well composed thee. Thy father's moral parts
Mayst thou inherit too! Welcome to Paris.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: My thanks and duty are your majesty's.

units.speaker: KING
lines: I would I had that corporal soundness now,
As when thy father and myself in friendship
First tried our soldiership! He did look far
Into the service of the time and was
Discipled of the bravest: he lasted long;
But on us both did haggish age steal on
And wore us out of act. It much repairs me
To talk of your good father. In his youth
He had the wit which I can well observe
To-day in our young lords; but they may jest
Till their own scorn return to them unnoted
Ere they can hide their levity in honour;
So like a courtier, contempt nor bitterness
Were in his pride or sharpness; if they were,
His equal had awaked them, and his honour,
Clock to itself, knew the true minute when
Exception bid him speak, and at this time
His tongue obey'd his hand: who were below him
He used as creatures of another place
And bow'd his eminent top to their low ranks,
Making them proud of his humility,
In their poor praise he humbled. Such a man
Might be a copy to these younger times;
Which, follow'd well, would demonstrate them now
But goers backward.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: His good remembrance, sir,
Lies richer in your thoughts than on his tomb;
So in approof lives not his epitaph
As in your royal speech.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Would I were with him! He would always say--
Methinks I hear him now; his plausive words
He scatter'd not in ears, but grafted them,
To grow there and to bear,--'Let me not live,'--
This his good melancholy oft began,
On the catastrophe and heel of pastime,
When it was out,--'Let me not live,' quoth he,
'After my flame lacks oil, to be the snuff
Of younger spirits, whose apprehensive senses
All but new things disdain; whose judgments are
Mere fathers of their garments; whose constancies
Expire before their fashions.' This he wish'd;
I after him do after him wish too,
Since I nor wax nor honey can bring home,
I quickly were dissolved from my hive,
To give some labourers room.

units.speaker: Second Lord
lines: You are loved, sir:
They that least lend it you shall lack you first.

units.speaker: KING
lines: I fill a place, I know't. How long is't, count,
Since the physician at your father's died?
He was much famed.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Some six months since, my lord.

units.speaker: KING
lines: If he were living, I would try him yet.
Lend me an arm; the rest have worn me out
With several applications; nature and sickness
Debate it at their leisure. Welcome, count;
My son's no dearer.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Thank your majesty.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: I will now hear; what say you of this gentlewoman?

units.speaker: Steward
lines: Madam, the care I have had to even your content, I
wish might be found in the calendar of my past
endeavours; for then we wound our modesty and make
foul the clearness of our deservings, when of
ourselves we publish them.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: What does this knave here? Get you gone, sirrah:
the complaints I have heard of you I do not all
believe: 'tis my slowness that I do not; for I know
you lack not folly to commit them, and have ability
enough to make such knaveries yours.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: 'Tis not unknown to you, madam, I am a poor fellow.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Well, sir.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: No, madam, 'tis not so well that I am poor, though
many of the rich are damned: but, if I may have
your ladyship's good will to go to the world, Isbel
the woman and I will do as we may.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Wilt thou needs be a beggar?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: I do beg your good will in this case.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: In what case?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: In Isbel's case and mine own. Service is no
heritage: and I think I shall never have the
blessing of God till I have issue o' my body; for
they say barnes are blessings.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Tell me thy reason why thou wilt marry.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: My poor body, madam, requires it: I am driven on
by the flesh; and he must needs go that the devil drives.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Is this all your worship's reason?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: Faith, madam, I have other holy reasons such as they
are.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: May the world know them?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: I have been, madam, a wicked creature, as you and
all flesh and blood are; and, indeed, I do marry
that I may repent.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Thy marriage, sooner than thy wickedness.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: I am out o' friends, madam; and I hope to have
friends for my wife's sake.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Such friends are thine enemies, knave.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: You're shallow, madam, in great friends; for the
knaves come to do that for me which I am aweary of.
He that ears my land spares my team and gives me
leave to in the crop; if I be his cuckold, he's my
drudge: he that comforts my wife is the cherisher
of my flesh and blood; he that cherishes my flesh
and blood loves my flesh and blood; he that loves my
flesh and blood is my friend: ergo, he that kisses
my wife is my friend. If men could be contented to
be what they are, there were no fear in marriage;
for young Charbon the Puritan and old Poysam the
Papist, howsome'er their hearts are severed in
religion, their heads are both one; they may jowl
horns together, like any deer i' the herd.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Wilt thou ever be a foul-mouthed and calumnious knave?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: A prophet I, madam; and I speak the truth the next
way:
For I the ballad will repeat,
Which men full true shall find;
Your marriage comes by destiny,
Your cuckoo sings by kind.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Get you gone, sir; I'll talk with you more anon.

units.speaker: Steward
lines: May it please you, madam, that he bid Helen come to
you: of her I am to speak.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Sirrah, tell my gentlewoman I would speak with her;
Helen, I mean.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: Was this fair face the cause, quoth she,
Why the Grecians sacked Troy?
Fond done, done fond,
Was this King Priam's joy?
With that she sighed as she stood,
With that she sighed as she stood,
And gave this sentence then;
Among nine bad if one be good,
Among nine bad if one be good,
There's yet one good in ten.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: What, one good in ten? you corrupt the song, sirrah.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: One good woman in ten, madam; which is a purifying
o' the song: would God would serve the world so all
the year! we'ld find no fault with the tithe-woman,
if I were the parson. One in ten, quoth a'! An we
might have a good woman born but one every blazing
star, or at an earthquake, 'twould mend the lottery
well: a man may draw his heart out, ere a' pluck
one.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: You'll be gone, sir knave, and do as I command you.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: That man should be at woman's command, and yet no
hurt done! Though honesty be no puritan, yet it
will do no hurt; it will wear the surplice of
humility over the black gown of a big heart. I am
going, forsooth: the business is for Helen to come hither.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Well, now.

units.speaker: Steward
lines: I know, madam, you love your gentlewoman entirely.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Faith, I do: her father bequeathed her to me; and
she herself, without other advantage, may lawfully
make title to as much love as she finds: there is
more owing her than is paid; and more shall be paid
her than she'll demand.

units.speaker: Steward
lines: Madam, I was very late more near her than I think
she wished me: alone she was, and did communicate
to herself her own words to her own ears; she
thought, I dare vow for her, they touched not any
stranger sense. Her matter was, she loved your son:
Fortune, she said, was no goddess, that had put
such difference betwixt their two estates; Love no
god, that would not extend his might, only where
qualities were level; Dian no queen of virgins, that
would suffer her poor knight surprised, without
rescue in the first assault or ransom afterward.
This she delivered in the most bitter touch of
sorrow that e'er I heard virgin exclaim in: which I
held my duty speedily to acquaint you withal;
sithence, in the loss that may happen, it concerns
you something to know it.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: You have discharged this honestly; keep it to
yourself: many likelihoods informed me of this
before, which hung so tottering in the balance that
I could neither believe nor misdoubt. Pray you,
leave me: stall this in your bosom; and I thank you
for your honest care: I will speak with you further anon.
Even so it was with me when I was young:
If ever we are nature's, these are ours; this thorn
Doth to our rose of youth rightly belong;
Our blood to us, this to our blood is born;
It is the show and seal of nature's truth,
Where love's strong passion is impress'd in youth:
By our remembrances of days foregone,
Such were our faults, or then we thought them none.
Her eye is sick on't: I observe her now.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: What is your pleasure, madam?

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: You know, Helen,
I am a mother to you.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Mine honourable mistress.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Nay, a mother:
Why not a mother? When I said 'a mother,'
Methought you saw a serpent: what's in 'mother,'
That you start at it? I say, I am your mother;
And put you in the catalogue of those
That were enwombed mine: 'tis often seen
Adoption strives with nature and choice breeds
A native slip to us from foreign seeds:
You ne'er oppress'd me with a mother's groan,
Yet I express to you a mother's care:
God's mercy, maiden! does it curd thy blood
To say I am thy mother? What's the matter,
That this distemper'd messenger of wet,
The many-colour'd Iris, rounds thine eye?
Why? that you are my daughter?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: That I am not.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: I say, I am your mother.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Pardon, madam;
The Count Rousillon cannot be my brother:
I am from humble, he from honour'd name;
No note upon my parents, his all noble:
My master, my dear lord he is; and I
His servant live, and will his vassal die:
He must not be my brother.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Nor I your mother?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: You are my mother, madam; would you were,--
So that my lord your son were not my brother,--
Indeed my mother! or were you both our mothers,
I care no more for than I do for heaven,
So I were not his sister. Can't no other,
But, I your daughter, he must be my brother?

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Yes, Helen, you might be my daughter-in-law:
God shield you mean it not! daughter and mother
So strive upon your pulse. What, pale again?
My fear hath catch'd your fondness: now I see
The mystery of your loneliness, and find
Your salt tears' head: now to all sense 'tis gross
You love my son; invention is ashamed,
Against the proclamation of thy passion,
To say thou dost not: therefore tell me true;
But tell me then, 'tis so; for, look thy cheeks
Confess it, th' one to th' other; and thine eyes
See it so grossly shown in thy behaviors
That in their kind they speak it: only sin
And hellish obstinacy tie thy tongue,
That truth should be suspected. Speak, is't so?
If it be so, you have wound a goodly clew;
If it be not, forswear't: howe'er, I charge thee,
As heaven shall work in me for thine avail,
Tell me truly.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Good madam, pardon me!

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Do you love my son?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Your pardon, noble mistress!

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Love you my son?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Do not you love him, madam?

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Go not about; my love hath in't a bond,
Whereof the world takes note: come, come, disclose
The state of your affection; for your passions
Have to the full appeach'd.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Then, I confess,
Here on my knee, before high heaven and you,
That before you, and next unto high heaven,
I love your son.
My friends were poor, but honest; so's my love:
Be not offended; for it hurts not him
That he is loved of me: I follow him not
By any token of presumptuous suit;
Nor would I have him till I do deserve him;
Yet never know how that desert should be.
I know I love in vain, strive against hope;
Yet in this captious and intenible sieve
I still pour in the waters of my love
And lack not to lose still: thus, Indian-like,
Religious in mine error, I adore
The sun, that looks upon his worshipper,
But knows of him no more. My dearest madam,
Let not your hate encounter with my love
For loving where you do: but if yourself,
Whose aged honour cites a virtuous youth,
Did ever in so true a flame of liking
Wish chastely and love dearly, that your Dian
Was both herself and love: O, then, give pity
To her, whose state is such that cannot choose
But lend and give where she is sure to lose;
That seeks not to find that her search implies,
But riddle-like lives sweetly where she dies!

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Had you not lately an intent,--speak truly,--
To go to Paris?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Madam, I had.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Wherefore? tell true.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I will tell truth; by grace itself I swear.
You know my father left me some prescriptions
Of rare and proved effects, such as his reading
And manifest experience had collected
For general sovereignty; and that he will'd me
In heedfull'st reservation to bestow them,
As notes whose faculties inclusive were
More than they were in note: amongst the rest,
There is a remedy, approved, set down,
To cure the desperate languishings whereof
The king is render'd lost.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: This was your motive
For Paris, was it? speak.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: My lord your son made me to think of this;
Else Paris and the medicine and the king
Had from the conversation of my thoughts
Haply been absent then.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: But think you, Helen,
If you should tender your supposed aid,
He would receive it? he and his physicians
Are of a mind; he, that they cannot help him,
They, that they cannot help: how shall they credit
A poor unlearned virgin, when the schools,
Embowell'd of their doctrine, have left off
The danger to itself?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: There's something in't,
More than my father's skill, which was the greatest
Of his profession, that his good receipt
Shall for my legacy be sanctified
By the luckiest stars in heaven: and, would your honour
But give me leave to try success, I'ld venture
The well-lost life of mine on his grace's cure
By such a day and hour.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Dost thou believe't?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Ay, madam, knowingly.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Why, Helen, thou shalt have my leave and love,
Means and attendants and my loving greetings
To those of mine in court: I'll stay at home
And pray God's blessing into thy attempt:
Be gone to-morrow; and be sure of this,
What I can help thee to thou shalt not miss.

units.text: Flourish of cornets. Enter the KING, attended  with divers young Lords taking leave for the Florentine war; BERTRAM, and PAROLLES

units.speaker: KING
lines: Farewell, young lords; these warlike principles
Do not throw from you: and you, my lords, farewell:
Share the advice betwixt you; if both gain, all
The gift doth stretch itself as 'tis received,
And is enough for both.

units.speaker: First Lord
lines: 'Tis our hope, sir,
After well enter'd soldiers, to return
And find your grace in health.

units.speaker: KING
lines: No, no, it cannot be; and yet my heart
Will not confess he owes the malady
That doth my life besiege. Farewell, young lords;
Whether I live or die, be you the sons
Of worthy Frenchmen: let higher Italy,--
Those bated that inherit but the fall
Of the last monarchy,--see that you come
Not to woo honour, but to wed it; when
The bravest questant shrinks, find what you seek,
That fame may cry you loud: I say, farewell.

units.speaker: Second Lord
lines: Health, at your bidding, serve your majesty!

units.speaker: KING
lines: Those girls of Italy, take heed of them:
They say, our French lack language to deny,
If they demand: beware of being captives,
Before you serve.

units.speaker: Both
lines: Our hearts receive your warnings.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Farewell. Come hither to me.

units.speaker: First Lord
lines: O, my sweet lord, that you will stay behind us!

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: 'Tis not his fault, the spark.

units.speaker: Second Lord
lines: O, 'tis brave wars!

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Most admirable: I have seen those wars.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: I am commanded here, and kept a coil with
'Too young' and 'the next year' and ''tis too early.'

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: An thy mind stand to't, boy, steal away bravely.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: I shall stay here the forehorse to a smock,
Creaking my shoes on the plain masonry,
Till honour be bought up and no sword worn
But one to dance with! By heaven, I'll steal away.

units.speaker: First Lord
lines: There's honour in the theft.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Commit it, count.

units.speaker: Second Lord
lines: I am your accessary; and so, farewell.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: I grow to you, and our parting is a tortured body.

units.speaker: First Lord
lines: Farewell, captain.

units.speaker: Second Lord
lines: Sweet Monsieur Parolles!

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Noble heroes, my sword and yours are kin. Good
sparks and lustrous, a word, good metals: you shall
find in the regiment of the Spinii one Captain
Spurio, with his cicatrice, an emblem of war, here
on his sinister cheek; it was this very sword
entrenched it: say to him, I live; and observe his
reports for me.

units.speaker: First Lord
lines: We shall, noble captain.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Mars dote on you for his novices! what will ye do?

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Stay: the king.

units.text: Re-enter KING. BERTRAM and PAROLLES retire

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: [To BERTRAM]  Use a more spacious ceremony to the
noble lords; you have restrained yourself within the
list of too cold an adieu: be more expressive to
them: for they wear themselves in the cap of the
time, there do muster true gait, eat, speak, and
move under the influence of the most received star;
and though the devil lead the measure, such are to
be followed: after them, and take a more dilated farewell.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: And I will do so.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Worthy fellows; and like to prove most sinewy sword-men.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: [Kneeling]  Pardon, my lord, for me and for my tidings.

units.speaker: KING
lines: I'll fee thee to stand up.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Then here's a man stands, that has brought his pardon.
I would you had kneel'd, my lord, to ask me mercy,
And that at my bidding you could so stand up.

units.speaker: KING
lines: I would I had; so I had broke thy pate,
And ask'd thee mercy for't.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Good faith, across: but, my good lord 'tis thus;
Will you be cured of your infirmity?

units.speaker: KING
lines: No.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: O, will you eat no grapes, my royal fox?
Yes, but you will my noble grapes, an if
My royal fox could reach them: I have seen a medicine
That's able to breathe life into a stone,
Quicken a rock, and make you dance canary
With spritely fire and motion; whose simple touch,
Is powerful to araise King Pepin, nay,
To give great Charlemain a pen in's hand,
And write to her a love-line.

units.speaker: KING
lines: What 'her' is this?

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Why, Doctor She: my lord, there's one arrived,
If you will see her: now, by my faith and honour,
If seriously I may convey my thoughts
In this my light deliverance, I have spoke
With one that, in her sex, her years, profession,
Wisdom and constancy, hath amazed me more
Than I dare blame my weakness: will you see her
For that is her demand, and know her business?
That done, laugh well at me.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Now, good Lafeu,
Bring in the admiration; that we with thee
May spend our wonder too, or take off thine
By wondering how thou took'st it.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Nay, I'll fit you,
And not be all day neither.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Thus he his special nothing ever prologues.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Nay, come your ways.

units.speaker: KING
lines: This haste hath wings indeed.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Nay, come your ways:
This is his majesty; say your mind to him:
A traitor you do look like; but such traitors
His majesty seldom fears: I am Cressid's uncle,
That dare leave two together; fare you well.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Now, fair one, does your business follow us?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Ay, my good lord.
Gerard de Narbon was my father;
In what he did profess, well found.

units.speaker: KING
lines: I knew him.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: The rather will I spare my praises towards him:
Knowing him is enough. On's bed of death
Many receipts he gave me: chiefly one.
Which, as the dearest issue of his practise,
And of his old experience the oily darling,
He bade me store up, as a triple eye,
Safer than mine own two, more dear; I have so;
And hearing your high majesty is touch'd
With that malignant cause wherein the honour
Of my dear father's gift stands chief in power,
I come to tender it and my appliance
With all bound humbleness.

units.speaker: KING
lines: We thank you, maiden;
But may not be so credulous of cure,
When our most learned doctors leave us and
The congregated college have concluded
That labouring art can never ransom nature
From her inaidible estate; I say we must not
So stain our judgment, or corrupt our hope,
To prostitute our past-cure malady
To empirics, or to dissever so
Our great self and our credit, to esteem
A senseless help when help past sense we deem.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: My duty then shall pay me for my pains:
I will no more enforce mine office on you.
Humbly entreating from your royal thoughts
A modest one, to bear me back again.

units.speaker: KING
lines: I cannot give thee less, to be call'd grateful:
Thou thought'st to help me; and such thanks I give
As one near death to those that wish him live:
But what at full I know, thou know'st no part,
I knowing all my peril, thou no art.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: What I can do can do no hurt to try,
Since you set up your rest 'gainst remedy.
He that of greatest works is finisher
Oft does them by the weakest minister:
So holy writ in babes hath judgment shown,
When judges have been babes; great floods have flown
From simple sources, and great seas have dried
When miracles have by the greatest been denied.
Oft expectation fails and most oft there
Where most it promises, and oft it hits
Where hope is coldest and despair most fits.

units.speaker: KING
lines: I must not hear thee; fare thee well, kind maid;
Thy pains not used must by thyself be paid:
Proffers not took reap thanks for their reward.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Inspired merit so by breath is barr'd:
It is not so with Him that all things knows
As 'tis with us that square our guess by shows;
But most it is presumption in us when
The help of heaven we count the act of men.
Dear sir, to my endeavours give consent;
Of heaven, not me, make an experiment.
I am not an impostor that proclaim
Myself against the level of mine aim;
But know I think and think I know most sure
My art is not past power nor you past cure.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Are thou so confident? within what space
Hopest thou my cure?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: The great'st grace lending grace
Ere twice the horses of the sun shall bring
Their fiery torcher his diurnal ring,
Ere twice in murk and occidental damp
Moist Hesperus hath quench'd his sleepy lamp,
Or four and twenty times the pilot's glass
Hath told the thievish minutes how they pass,
What is infirm from your sound parts shall fly,
Health shall live free and sickness freely die.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Upon thy certainty and confidence
What darest thou venture?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Tax of impudence,
A strumpet's boldness, a divulged shame
Traduced by odious ballads: my maiden's name
Sear'd otherwise; nay, worse--if worse--extended
With vilest torture let my life be ended.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Methinks in thee some blessed spirit doth speak
His powerful sound within an organ weak:
And what impossibility would slay
In common sense, sense saves another way.
Thy life is dear; for all that life can rate
Worth name of life in thee hath estimate,
Youth, beauty, wisdom, courage, all
That happiness and prime can happy call:
Thou this to hazard needs must intimate
Skill infinite or monstrous desperate.
Sweet practiser, thy physic I will try,
That ministers thine own death if I die.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: If I break time, or flinch in property
Of what I spoke, unpitied let me die,
And well deserved: not helping, death's my fee;
But, if I help, what do you promise me?

units.speaker: KING
lines: Make thy demand.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: But will you make it even?

units.speaker: KING
lines: Ay, by my sceptre and my hopes of heaven.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Then shalt thou give me with thy kingly hand
What husband in thy power I will command:
Exempted be from me the arrogance
To choose from forth the royal blood of France,
My low and humble name to propagate
With any branch or image of thy state;
But such a one, thy vassal, whom I know
Is free for me to ask, thee to bestow.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Here is my hand; the premises observed,
Thy will by my performance shall be served:
So make the choice of thy own time, for I,
Thy resolved patient, on thee still rely.
More should I question thee, and more I must,
Though more to know could not be more to trust,
From whence thou camest, how tended on: but rest
Unquestion'd welcome and undoubted blest.
Give me some help here, ho! If thou proceed
As high as word, my deed shall match thy meed.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Come on, sir; I shall now put you to the height of
your breeding.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: I will show myself highly fed and lowly taught: I
know my business is but to the court.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: To the court! why, what place make you special,
when you put off that with such contempt? But to the court!

units.speaker: Clown
lines: Truly, madam, if God have lent a man any manners, he
may easily put it off at court: he that cannot make
a leg, put off's cap, kiss his hand and say nothing,
has neither leg, hands, lip, nor cap; and indeed
such a fellow, to say precisely, were not for the
court; but for me, I have an answer will serve all
men.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Marry, that's a bountiful answer that fits all
questions.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: It is like a barber's chair that fits all buttocks,
the pin-buttock, the quatch-buttock, the brawn
buttock, or any buttock.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Will your answer serve fit to all questions?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: As fit as ten groats is for the hand of an attorney,
as your French crown for your taffeta punk, as Tib's
rush for Tom's forefinger, as a pancake for Shrove
Tuesday, a morris for May-day, as the nail to his
hole, the cuckold to his horn, as a scolding queen
to a wrangling knave, as the nun's lip to the
friar's mouth, nay, as the pudding to his skin.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Have you, I say, an answer of such fitness for all
questions?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: From below your duke to beneath your constable, it
will fit any question.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: It must be an answer of most monstrous size that
must fit all demands.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: But a trifle neither, in good faith, if the learned
should speak truth of it: here it is, and all that
belongs to't. Ask me if I am a courtier: it shall
do you no harm to learn.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: To be young again, if we could: I will be a fool in
question, hoping to be the wiser by your answer. I
pray you, sir, are you a courtier?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: O Lord, sir! There's a simple putting off. More,
more, a hundred of them.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Sir, I am a poor friend of yours, that loves you.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: O Lord, sir! Thick, thick, spare not me.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: I think, sir, you can eat none of this homely meat.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: O Lord, sir! Nay, put me to't, I warrant you.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: You were lately whipped, sir, as I think.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: O Lord, sir! spare not me.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Do you cry, 'O Lord, sir!' at your whipping, and
'spare not me?' Indeed your 'O Lord, sir!' is very
sequent to your whipping: you would answer very well
to a whipping, if you were but bound to't.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: I ne'er had worse luck in my life in my 'O Lord,
sir!' I see things may serve long, but not serve ever.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: I play the noble housewife with the time
To entertain't so merrily with a fool.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: O Lord, sir! why, there't serves well again.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: An end, sir; to your business. Give Helen this,
And urge her to a present answer back:
Commend me to my kinsmen and my son:
This is not much.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: Not much commendation to them.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Not much employment for you: you understand me?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: Most fruitfully: I am there before my legs.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Haste you again.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: They say miracles are past; and we have our
philosophical persons, to make modern and familiar,
things supernatural and causeless. Hence is it that
we make trifles of terrors, ensconcing ourselves
into seeming knowledge, when we should submit
ourselves to an unknown fear.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Why, 'tis the rarest argument of wonder that hath
shot out in our latter times.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: And so 'tis.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: To be relinquish'd of the artists,--

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: So I say.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Both of Galen and Paracelsus.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: So I say.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Of all the learned and authentic fellows,--

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Right; so I say.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: That gave him out incurable,--

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Why, there 'tis; so say I too.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Not to be helped,--

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Right; as 'twere, a man assured of a--

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Uncertain life, and sure death.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Just, you say well; so would I have said.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: I may truly say, it is a novelty to the world.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: It is, indeed: if you will have it in showing, you
shall read it in--what do you call there?

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: A showing of a heavenly effect in an earthly actor.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: That's it; I would have said the very same.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Why, your dolphin is not lustier: 'fore me,
I speak in respect--

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Nay, 'tis strange, 'tis very strange, that is the
brief and the tedious of it; and he's of a most
facinerious spirit that will not acknowledge it to be the--

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Very hand of heaven.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Ay, so I say.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: In a most weak--
and debile minister, great power, great
transcendence: which should, indeed, give us a
further use to be made than alone the recovery of
the king, as to be--
generally thankful.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: I would have said it; you say well. Here comes the king.

units.text: Enter KING, HELENA, and Attendants. LAFEU and PAROLLES retire

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Lustig, as the Dutchman says: I'll like a maid the
better, whilst I have a tooth in my head: why, he's
able to lead her a coranto.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Mort du vinaigre! is not this Helen?

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: 'Fore God, I think so.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Go, call before me all the lords in court.
Sit, my preserver, by thy patient's side;
And with this healthful hand, whose banish'd sense
Thou hast repeal'd, a second time receive
The confirmation of my promised gift,
Which but attends thy naming.
Fair maid, send forth thine eye: this youthful parcel
Of noble bachelors stand at my bestowing,
O'er whom both sovereign power and father's voice
I have to use: thy frank election make;
Thou hast power to choose, and they none to forsake.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: To each of you one fair and virtuous mistress
Fall, when Love please! marry, to each, but one!

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: I'ld give bay Curtal and his furniture,
My mouth no more were broken than these boys',
And writ as little beard.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Peruse them well:
Not one of those but had a noble father.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Gentlemen,
Heaven hath through me restored the king to health.

units.speaker: All
lines: We understand it, and thank heaven for you.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I am a simple maid, and therein wealthiest,
That I protest I simply am a maid.
Please it your majesty, I have done already:
The blushes in my cheeks thus whisper me,
'We blush that thou shouldst choose; but, be refused,
Let the white death sit on thy cheek for ever;
We'll ne'er come there again.'

units.speaker: KING
lines: Make choice; and, see,
Who shuns thy love shuns all his love in me.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Now, Dian, from thy altar do I fly,
And to imperial Love, that god most high,
Do my sighs stream. Sir, will you hear my suit?

units.speaker: First Lord
lines: And grant it.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Thanks, sir; all the rest is mute.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: I had rather be in this choice than throw ames-ace
for my life.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: The honour, sir, that flames in your fair eyes,
Before I speak, too threateningly replies:
Love make your fortunes twenty times above
Her that so wishes and her humble love!

units.speaker: Second Lord
lines: No better, if you please.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: My wish receive,
Which great Love grant! and so, I take my leave.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Do all they deny her? An they were sons of mine,
I'd have them whipped; or I would send them to the
Turk, to make eunuchs of.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Be not afraid that I your hand should take;
I'll never do you wrong for your own sake:
Blessing upon your vows! and in your bed
Find fairer fortune, if you ever wed!

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: These boys are boys of ice, they'll none have her:
sure, they are bastards to the English; the French
ne'er got 'em.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: You are too young, too happy, and too good,
To make yourself a son out of my blood.

units.speaker: Fourth Lord
lines: Fair one, I think not so.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: There's one grape yet; I am sure thy father drunk
wine: but if thou be'st not an ass, I am a youth
of fourteen; I have known thee already.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: [To BERTRAM]  I dare not say I take you; but I give
Me and my service, ever whilst I live,
Into your guiding power. This is the man.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Why, then, young Bertram, take her; she's thy wife.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: My wife, my liege! I shall beseech your highness,
In such a business give me leave to use
The help of mine own eyes.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Know'st thou not, Bertram,
What she has done for me?

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Yes, my good lord;
But never hope to know why I should marry her.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Thou know'st she has raised me from my sickly bed.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: But follows it, my lord, to bring me down
Must answer for your raising? I know her well:
She had her breeding at my father's charge.
A poor physician's daughter my wife! Disdain
Rather corrupt me ever!

units.speaker: KING
lines: 'Tis only title thou disdain'st in her, the which
I can build up. Strange is it that our bloods,
Of colour, weight, and heat, pour'd all together,
Would quite confound distinction, yet stand off
In differences so mighty. If she be
All that is virtuous, save what thou dislikest,
A poor physician's daughter, thou dislikest
Of virtue for the name: but do not so:
From lowest place when virtuous things proceed,
The place is dignified by the doer's deed:
Where great additions swell's, and virtue none,
It is a dropsied honour. Good alone
Is good without a name. Vileness is so:
The property by what it is should go,
Not by the title. She is young, wise, fair;
In these to nature she's immediate heir,
And these breed honour: that is honour's scorn,
Which challenges itself as honour's born
And is not like the sire: honours thrive,
When rather from our acts we them derive
Than our foregoers: the mere word's a slave
Debosh'd on every tomb, on every grave
A lying trophy, and as oft is dumb
Where dust and damn'd oblivion is the tomb
Of honour'd bones indeed. What should be said?
If thou canst like this creature as a maid,
I can create the rest: virtue and she
Is her own dower; honour and wealth from me.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: I cannot love her, nor will strive to do't.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Thou wrong'st thyself, if thou shouldst strive to choose.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: That you are well restored, my lord, I'm glad:
Let the rest go.

units.speaker: KING
lines: My honour's at the stake; which to defeat,
I must produce my power. Here, take her hand,
Proud scornful boy, unworthy this good gift;
That dost in vile misprision shackle up
My love and her desert; that canst not dream,
We, poising us in her defective scale,
Shall weigh thee to the beam; that wilt not know,
It is in us to plant thine honour where
We please to have it grow. Cheque thy contempt:
Obey our will, which travails in thy good:
Believe not thy disdain, but presently
Do thine own fortunes that obedient right
Which both thy duty owes and our power claims;
Or I will throw thee from my care for ever
Into the staggers and the careless lapse
Of youth and ignorance; both my revenge and hate
Loosing upon thee, in the name of justice,
Without all terms of pity. Speak; thine answer.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Pardon, my gracious lord; for I submit
My fancy to your eyes: when I consider
What great creation and what dole of honour
Flies where you bid it, I find that she, which late
Was in my nobler thoughts most base, is now
The praised of the king; who, so ennobled,
Is as 'twere born so.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Take her by the hand,
And tell her she is thine: to whom I promise
A counterpoise, if not to thy estate
A balance more replete.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: I take her hand.

units.speaker: KING
lines: Good fortune and the favour of the king
Smile upon this contract; whose ceremony
Shall seem expedient on the now-born brief,
And be perform'd to-night: the solemn feast
Shall more attend upon the coming space,
Expecting absent friends. As thou lovest her,
Thy love's to me religious; else, does err.

units.text: Exeunt all but LAFEU and PAROLLES

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: [Advancing]  Do you hear, monsieur? a word with you.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Your pleasure, sir?

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Your lord and master did well to make his
recantation.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Recantation! My lord! my master!

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Ay; is it not a language I speak?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: A most harsh one, and not to be understood without
bloody succeeding. My master!

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Are you companion to the Count Rousillon?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: To any count, to all counts, to what is man.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: To what is count's man: count's master is of
another style.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: You are too old, sir; let it satisfy you, you are too old.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: I must tell thee, sirrah, I write man; to which
title age cannot bring thee.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: What I dare too well do, I dare not do.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: I did think thee, for two ordinaries, to be a pretty
wise fellow; thou didst make tolerable vent of thy
travel; it might pass: yet the scarfs and the
bannerets about thee did manifoldly dissuade me from
believing thee a vessel of too great a burthen. I
have now found thee; when I lose thee again, I care
not: yet art thou good for nothing but taking up; and
that thou't scarce worth.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Hadst thou not the privilege of antiquity upon thee,--

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Do not plunge thyself too far in anger, lest thou
hasten thy trial; which if--Lord have mercy on thee
for a hen! So, my good window of lattice, fare thee
well: thy casement I need not open, for I look
through thee. Give me thy hand.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: My lord, you give me most egregious indignity.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Ay, with all my heart; and thou art worthy of it.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: I have not, my lord, deserved it.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Yes, good faith, every dram of it; and I will not
bate thee a scruple.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Well, I shall be wiser.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Even as soon as thou canst, for thou hast to pull at
a smack o' the contrary. If ever thou be'st bound
in thy scarf and beaten, thou shalt find what it is
to be proud of thy bondage. I have a desire to hold
my acquaintance with thee, or rather my knowledge,
that I may say in the default, he is a man I know.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: My lord, you do me most insupportable vexation.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: I would it were hell-pains for thy sake, and my poor
doing eternal: for doing I am past: as I will by
thee, in what motion age will give me leave.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Well, thou hast a son shall take this disgrace off
me; scurvy, old, filthy, scurvy lord! Well, I must
be patient; there is no fettering of authority.
I'll beat him, by my life, if I can meet him with
any convenience, an he were double and double a
lord. I'll have no more pity of his age than I
would of--I'll beat him, an if I could but meet him again.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Sirrah, your lord and master's married; there's news
for you: you have a new mistress.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: I most unfeignedly beseech your lordship to make
some reservation of your wrongs: he is my good
lord: whom I serve above is my master.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Who? God?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Ay, sir.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: The devil it is that's thy master. Why dost thou
garter up thy arms o' this fashion? dost make hose of
sleeves? do other servants so? Thou wert best set
thy lower part where thy nose stands. By mine
honour, if I were but two hours younger, I'ld beat
thee: methinks, thou art a general offence, and
every man should beat thee: I think thou wast
created for men to breathe themselves upon thee.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: This is hard and undeserved measure, my lord.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Go to, sir; you were beaten in Italy for picking a
kernel out of a pomegranate; you are a vagabond and
no true traveller: you are more saucy with lords
and honourable personages than the commission of your
birth and virtue gives you heraldry. You are not
worth another word, else I'ld call you knave. I leave you.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Good, very good; it is so then: good, very good;
let it be concealed awhile.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Undone, and forfeited to cares for ever!

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: What's the matter, sweet-heart?

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Although before the solemn priest I have sworn,
I will not bed her.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: What, what, sweet-heart?

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: O my Parolles, they have married me!
I'll to the Tuscan wars, and never bed her.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: France is a dog-hole, and it no more merits
The tread of a man's foot: to the wars!

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: There's letters from my mother: what the import is,
I know not yet.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Ay, that would be known. To the wars, my boy, to the wars!
He wears his honour in a box unseen,
That hugs his kicky-wicky here at home,
Spending his manly marrow in her arms,
Which should sustain the bound and high curvet
Of Mars's fiery steed. To other regions
France is a stable; we that dwell in't jades;
Therefore, to the war!

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: It shall be so: I'll send her to my house,
Acquaint my mother with my hate to her,
And wherefore I am fled; write to the king
That which I durst not speak; his present gift
Shall furnish me to those Italian fields,
Where noble fellows strike: war is no strife
To the dark house and the detested wife.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Will this capriccio hold in thee? art sure?

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Go with me to my chamber, and advise me.
I'll send her straight away: to-morrow
I'll to the wars, she to her single sorrow.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Why, these balls bound; there's noise in it. 'Tis hard:
A young man married is a man that's marr'd:
Therefore away, and leave her bravely; go:
The king has done you wrong: but, hush, 'tis so.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: My mother greets me kindly; is she well?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: She is not well; but yet she has her health: she's
very merry; but yet she is not well: but thanks be
given, she's very well and wants nothing i', the
world; but yet she is not well.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: If she be very well, what does she ail, that she's
not very well?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: Truly, she's very well indeed, but for two things.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: What two things?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: One, that she's not in heaven, whither God send her
quickly! the other that she's in earth, from whence
God send her quickly!

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Bless you, my fortunate lady!

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I hope, sir, I have your good will to have mine own
good fortunes.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: You had my prayers to lead them on; and to keep them
on, have them still. O, my knave, how does my old lady?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: So that you had her wrinkles and I her money,
I would she did as you say.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Why, I say nothing.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: Marry, you are the wiser man; for many a man's
tongue shakes out his master's undoing: to say
nothing, to do nothing, to know nothing, and to have
nothing, is to be a great part of your title; which
is within a very little of nothing.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Away! thou'rt a knave.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: You should have said, sir, before a knave thou'rt a
knave; that's, before me thou'rt a knave: this had
been truth, sir.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Go to, thou art a witty fool; I have found thee.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: Did you find me in yourself, sir? or were you
taught to find me? The search, sir, was profitable;
and much fool may you find in you, even to the
world's pleasure and the increase of laughter.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: A good knave, i' faith, and well fed.
Madam, my lord will go away to-night;
A very serious business calls on him.
The great prerogative and rite of love,
Which, as your due, time claims, he does acknowledge;
But puts it off to a compell'd restraint;
Whose want, and whose delay, is strew'd with sweets,
Which they distil now in the curbed time,
To make the coming hour o'erflow with joy
And pleasure drown the brim.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: What's his will else?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: That you will take your instant leave o' the king
And make this haste as your own good proceeding,
Strengthen'd with what apology you think
May make it probable need.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: What more commands he?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: That, having this obtain'd, you presently
Attend his further pleasure.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: In every thing I wait upon his will.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: I shall report it so.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I pray you.
Come, sirrah.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: But I hope your lordship thinks not him a soldier.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Yes, my lord, and of very valiant approof.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: You have it from his own deliverance.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: And by other warranted testimony.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Then my dial goes not true: I took this lark for a bunting.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: I do assure you, my lord, he is very great in
knowledge and accordingly valiant.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: I have then sinned against his experience and
transgressed against his valour; and my state that
way is dangerous, since I cannot yet find in my
heart to repent. Here he comes: I pray you, make
us friends; I will pursue the amity.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: [To BERTRAM]  These things shall be done, sir.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: Pray you, sir, who's his tailor?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Sir?

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: O, I know him well, I, sir; he, sir, 's a good
workman, a very good tailor.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: [Aside to PAROLLES]  Is she gone to the king?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: She is.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Will she away to-night?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: As you'll have her.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: I have writ my letters, casketed my treasure,
Given order for our horses; and to-night,
When I should take possession of the bride,
End ere I do begin.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: A good traveller is something at the latter end of a
dinner; but one that lies three thirds and uses a
known truth to pass a thousand nothings with, should
be once heard and thrice beaten. God save you, captain.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Is there any unkindness between my lord and you, monsieur?

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: I know not how I have deserved to run into my lord's
displeasure.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: You have made shift to run into 't, boots and spurs
and all, like him that leaped into the custard; and
out of it you'll run again, rather than suffer
question for your residence.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: It may be you have mistaken him, my lord.

units.speaker: LAFEU
lines: And shall do so ever, though I took him at 's
prayers. Fare you well, my lord; and believe this
of me, there can be no kernel in this light nut; the
soul of this man is his clothes. Trust him not in
matter of heavy consequence; I have kept of them
tame, and know their natures. Farewell, monsieur:
I have spoken better of you than you have or will to
deserve at my hand; but we must do good against evil.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: An idle lord. I swear.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: I think so.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Why, do you not know him?

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Yes, I do know him well, and common speech
Gives him a worthy pass. Here comes my clog.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I have, sir, as I was commanded from you,
Spoke with the king and have procured his leave
For present parting; only he desires
Some private speech with you.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: I shall obey his will.
You must not marvel, Helen, at my course,
Which holds not colour with the time, nor does
The ministration and required office
On my particular. Prepared I was not
For such a business; therefore am I found
So much unsettled: this drives me to entreat you
That presently you take our way for home;
And rather muse than ask why I entreat you,
For my respects are better than they seem
And my appointments have in them a need
Greater than shows itself at the first view
To you that know them not. This to my mother:
'Twill be two days ere I shall see you, so
I leave you to your wisdom.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Sir, I can nothing say,
But that I am your most obedient servant.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Come, come, no more of that.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: And ever shall
With true observance seek to eke out that
Wherein toward me my homely stars have fail'd
To equal my great fortune.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Let that go:
My haste is very great: farewell; hie home.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Pray, sir, your pardon.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Well, what would you say?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I am not worthy of the wealth I owe,
Nor dare I say 'tis mine, and yet it is;
But, like a timorous thief, most fain would steal
What law does vouch mine own.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: What would you have?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Something; and scarce so much: nothing, indeed.
I would not tell you what I would, my lord:
Faith yes;
Strangers and foes do sunder, and not kiss.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: I pray you, stay not, but in haste to horse.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I shall not break your bidding, good my lord.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Where are my other men, monsieur? Farewell.
Go thou toward home; where I will never come
Whilst I can shake my sword or hear the drum.
Away, and for our flight.

units.speaker: PAROLLES
lines: Bravely, coragio!

units.text: Flourish. Enter the DUKE of Florence attended; the two Frenchmen, with a troop of soldiers.

units.speaker: DUKE
lines: So that from point to point now have you heard
The fundamental reasons of this war,
Whose great decision hath much blood let forth
And more thirsts after.

units.speaker: First Lord
lines: Holy seems the quarrel
Upon your grace's part; black and fearful
On the opposer.

units.speaker: DUKE
lines: Therefore we marvel much our cousin France
Would in so just a business shut his bosom
Against our borrowing prayers.

units.speaker: Second Lord
lines: Good my lord,
The reasons of our state I cannot yield,
But like a common and an outward man,
That the great figure of a council frames
By self-unable motion: therefore dare not
Say what I think of it, since I have found
Myself in my incertain grounds to fail
As often as I guess'd.

units.speaker: DUKE
lines: Be it his pleasure.

units.speaker: First Lord
lines: But I am sure the younger of our nature,
That surfeit on their ease, will day by day
Come here for physic.

units.speaker: DUKE
lines: Welcome shall they be;
And all the honours that can fly from us
Shall on them settle. You know your places well;
When better fall, for your avails they fell:
To-morrow to the field.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: It hath happened all as I would have had it, save
that he comes not along with her.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: By my troth, I take my young lord to be a very
melancholy man.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: By what observance, I pray you?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: Why, he will look upon his boot and sing; mend the
ruff and sing; ask questions and sing; pick his
teeth and sing. I know a man that had this trick of
melancholy sold a goodly manor for a song.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Let me see what he writes, and when he means to come.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: I have no mind to Isbel since I was at court: our
old ling and our Isbels o' the country are nothing
like your old ling and your Isbels o' the court:
the brains of my Cupid's knocked out, and I begin to
love, as an old man loves money, with no stomach.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: What have we here?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: E'en that you have there.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: [Reads]  I have sent you a daughter-in-law: she hath
recovered the king, and undone me. I have wedded
her, not bedded her; and sworn to make the 'not'
eternal. You shall hear I am run away: know it
before the report come. If there be breadth enough
in the world, I will hold a long distance. My duty
to you.	Your unfortunate son,
BERTRAM.
This is not well, rash and unbridled boy.
To fly the favours of so good a king;
To pluck his indignation on thy head
By the misprising of a maid too virtuous
For the contempt of empire.

units.speaker: Clown
lines: O madam, yonder is heavy news within between two
soldiers and my young lady!

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: What is the matter?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: Nay, there is some comfort in the news, some
comfort; your son will not be killed so soon as I
thought he would.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Why should he be killed?

units.speaker: Clown
lines: So say I, madam, if he run away, as I hear he does:
the danger is in standing to't; that's the loss of
men, though it be the getting of children. Here
they come will tell you more: for my part, I only
hear your son was run away.

units.speaker: First Gentleman
lines: Save you, good madam.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Madam, my lord is gone, for ever gone.

units.speaker: Second Gentleman
lines: Do not say so.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Think upon patience. Pray you, gentlemen,
I have felt so many quirks of joy and grief,
That the first face of neither, on the start,
Can woman me unto't: where is my son, I pray you?

units.speaker: Second Gentleman
lines: Madam, he's gone to serve the duke of Florence:
We met him thitherward; for thence we came,
And, after some dispatch in hand at court,
Thither we bend again.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Look on his letter, madam; here's my passport.
When thou canst get the ring upon my finger which
never shall come off, and show me a child begotten
of thy body that I am father to, then call me
husband: but in such a 'then' I write a 'never.'
This is a dreadful sentence.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Brought you this letter, gentlemen?

units.speaker: First Gentleman
lines: Ay, madam;
And for the contents' sake are sorry for our pain.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: I prithee, lady, have a better cheer;
If thou engrossest all the griefs are thine,
Thou robb'st me of a moiety: he was my son;
But I do wash his name out of my blood,
And thou art all my child. Towards Florence is he?

units.speaker: Second Gentleman
lines: Ay, madam.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: And to be a soldier?

units.speaker: Second Gentleman
lines: Such is his noble purpose; and believe 't,
The duke will lay upon him all the honour
That good convenience claims.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Return you thither?

units.speaker: First Gentleman
lines: Ay, madam, with the swiftest wing of speed.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: [Reads]  Till I have no wife I have nothing in France.
'Tis bitter.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Find you that there?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Ay, madam.

units.speaker: First Gentleman
lines: 'Tis but the boldness of his hand, haply, which his
heart was not consenting to.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Nothing in France, until he have no wife!
There's nothing here that is too good for him
But only she; and she deserves a lord
That twenty such rude boys might tend upon
And call her hourly mistress. Who was with him?

units.speaker: First Gentleman
lines: A servant only, and a gentleman
Which I have sometime known.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Parolles, was it not?

units.speaker: First Gentleman
lines: Ay, my good lady, he.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: A very tainted fellow, and full of wickedness.
My son corrupts a well-derived nature
With his inducement.

units.speaker: First Gentleman
lines: Indeed, good lady,
The fellow has a deal of that too much,
Which holds him much to have.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: You're welcome, gentlemen.
I will entreat you, when you see my son,
To tell him that his sword can never win
The honour that he loses: more I'll entreat you
Written to bear along.

units.speaker: Second Gentleman
lines: We serve you, madam,
In that and all your worthiest affairs.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Not so, but as we change our courtesies.
Will you draw near!

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: 'Till I have no wife, I have nothing in France.'
Nothing in France, until he has no wife!
Thou shalt have none, Rousillon, none in France;
Then hast thou all again. Poor lord! is't I
That chase thee from thy country and expose
Those tender limbs of thine to the event
Of the none-sparing war? and is it I
That drive thee from the sportive court, where thou
Wast shot at with fair eyes, to be the mark
Of smoky muskets? O you leaden messengers,
That ride upon the violent speed of fire,
Fly with false aim; move the still-peering air,
That sings with piercing; do not touch my lord.
Whoever shoots at him, I set him there;
Whoever charges on his forward breast,
I am the caitiff that do hold him to't;
And, though I kill him not, I am the cause
His death was so effected: better 'twere
I met the ravin lion when he roar'd
With sharp constraint of hunger; better 'twere
That all the miseries which nature owes
Were mine at once. No, come thou home, Rousillon,
Whence honour but of danger wins a scar,
As oft it loses all: I will be gone;
My being here it is that holds thee hence:
Shall I stay here to do't?  no, no, although
The air of paradise did fan the house
And angels officed all: I will be gone,
That pitiful rumour may report my flight,
To consolate thine ear. Come, night; end, day!
For with the dark, poor thief, I'll steal away.

units.text: Flourish. Enter the DUKE of Florence, BERTRAM, PAROLLES, Soldiers, Drum, and Trumpets

units.speaker: DUKE
lines: The general of our horse thou art; and we,
Great in our hope, lay our best love and credence
Upon thy promising fortune.

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: Sir, it is
A charge too heavy for my strength, but yet
We'll strive to bear it for your worthy sake
To the extreme edge of hazard.

units.speaker: DUKE
lines: Then go thou forth;
And fortune play upon thy prosperous helm,
As thy auspicious mistress!

units.speaker: BERTRAM
lines: This very day,
Great Mars, I put myself into thy file:
Make me but like my thoughts, and I shall prove
A lover of thy drum, hater of love.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Alas! and would you take the letter of her?
Might you not know she would do as she has done,
By sending me a letter? Read it again.

units.speaker: Steward
lines: [Reads]
I am Saint Jaques' pilgrim, thither gone:
Ambitious love hath so in me offended,
That barefoot plod I the cold ground upon,
With sainted vow my faults to have amended.
Write, write, that from the bloody course of war
My dearest master, your dear son, may hie:
Bless him at home in peace, whilst I from far
His name with zealous fervor sanctify:
His taken labours bid him me forgive;
I, his despiteful Juno, sent him forth
From courtly friends, with camping foes to live,
Where death and danger dogs the heels of worth:
He is too good and fair for death and me:
Whom I myself embrace, to set him free.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: Ah, what sharp stings are in her mildest words!
Rinaldo, you did never lack advice so much,
As letting her pass so: had I spoke with her,
I could have well diverted her intents,
Which thus she hath prevented.

units.speaker: Steward
lines: Pardon me, madam:
If I had given you this at over-night,
She might have been o'erta'en; and yet she writes,
Pursuit would be but vain.

units.speaker: COUNTESS
lines: What angel shall
Bless this unworthy husband? he cannot thrive,
Unless her prayers, whom heaven delights to hear
And loves to grant, reprieve him from the wrath
Of greatest justice. Write, write, Rinaldo,
To this unworthy husband of his wife;
Let every word weigh heavy of her worth
That he does weigh too light: my greatest grief.
Though little he do feel it, set down sharply.
Dispatch the most convenient messenger:
When haply he shall hear that she is gone,
He will return; and hope I may that she,
Hearing so much, will speed her foot again,
Led hither by pure love: which of them both
Is dearest to me. I have no skill in sense
To make distinction: provide this messenger:
My heart is heavy and mine age is weak;
Grief would have tears, and sorrow bids me speak.

scenes.setting: Florence. Without the walls. A tucket afar off.

units.text: Enter an old Widow of Florence, DIANA, VIOLENTA, and MARIANA, with other Citizens

units.speaker: Widow
lines: Nay, come; for if they do approach the city, we
shall lose all the sight.

units.speaker: DIANA
lines: They say the French count has done most honourable service.

units.speaker: Widow
lines: It is reported that he has taken their greatest
commander; and that with his own hand he slew the
duke's brother.
We have lost our labour; they are gone a contrary
way: hark! you may know by their trumpets.

units.speaker: MARIANA
lines: Come, let's return again, and suffice ourselves with
the report of it. Well, Diana, take heed of this
French earl: the honour of a maid is her name; and
no legacy is so rich as honesty.

units.speaker: Widow
lines: I have told my neighbour how you have been solicited
by a gentleman his companion.

units.speaker: MARIANA
lines: I know that knave; hang him! one Parolles: a
filthy officer he is in those suggestions for the
young earl. Beware of them, Diana; their promises,
enticements, oaths, tokens, and all these engines of
lust, are not the things they go under: many a maid
hath been seduced by them; and the misery is,
example, that so terrible shows in the wreck of
maidenhood, cannot for all that dissuade succession,
but that they are limed with the twigs that threaten
them. I hope I need not to advise you further; but
I hope your own grace will keep you where you are,
though there were no further danger known but the
modesty which is so lost.

units.speaker: DIANA
lines: You shall not need to fear me.

units.speaker: Widow
lines: I hope so.

units.text: Enter HELENA, disguised like a Pilgrim

lines: Look, here comes a pilgrim: I know she will lie at
my house; thither they send one another: I'll
question her. God save you, pilgrim! whither are you bound?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: To Saint Jaques le Grand.
Where do the palmers lodge, I do beseech you?

units.speaker: Widow
lines: At the Saint Francis here beside the port.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Is this the way?

units.speaker: Widow
lines: Ay, marry, is't.
Hark you! they come this way.
If you will tarry, holy pilgrim,
But till the troops come by,
I will conduct you where you shall be lodged;
The rather, for I think I know your hostess
As ample as myself.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: Is it yourself?

units.speaker: Widow
lines: If you shall please so, pilgrim.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I thank you, and will stay upon your leisure.

units.speaker: Widow
lines: You came, I think, from France?

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: I did so.

units.speaker: Widow
lines: Here you shall see a countryman of yours
That has done worthy service.

units.speaker: HELENA
lines: His name, I pray you.

units.speaker: DIANA
